title: Modular Architecture
objectives: Split a monolith into independent packages
Enforce module boundaries and public APIs
Speed up builds and enable team ownership

sections.markdown: Modular architecture splits the codebase into independent packages (modules) with explicit dependencies. In Flutter this is typically a multi-package repo — an `app/` module plus `core/`, `ui/`, `feature_auth/`, `feature_cart/` and so on.

sections.code:
packages/
  core/           (network, storage, config)
  design_system/  (theme, tokens, shared widgets)
  feature_auth/   (public: AuthApi, private: impl)
  feature_cart/
  app/            (composes features, wires DI)

sections.markdown: The main win of modular architecture is **compiler-enforced boundaries**: package B cannot import a private symbol of package A. That's stronger than any lint rule and scales to many teams working in parallel.
Modularizing too early. For a small team/app, one package with strict folders is enough. Modularize when build times or team coordination become a bottleneck.

flashcards.back: Import boundaries — a package can only see another's public API.

flashcards.front: Typical modules in a Flutter monorepo?
flashcards.back: core, design_system, feature_X packages, and an app module that composes them.
When build times or team coordination hurt — not day one.

quiz.explain: Modules can only expose their public API.

quiz.q: A common Flutter modular layout has…
quiz.explain: Feature packages composed by an app module.

quiz.q: The main risk of early modularization is…
quiz.explain: You pay setup cost before the wins arrive.

challenges.text: Extract a feature into its own package with a small public API.
Set up a monorepo (melos) with core + design_system + one feature package.
Break a circular dependency between two features by introducing a shared abstraction.
Add CI that builds only affected packages on push.

interviewQuestions: What is modular architecture and why use it?
